Sakoku period of Japan

# Sakoku period of Japan

### How it shaped it's history and culture

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Jul 08, 2021

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Share

To understand this, you need to look at the period they called [Sakoku](https://www.quora.com/How-has-the-isolation-from-Western-culture-shaped-modern-day-Japan-In-what-ways-would-Japan-be-different-today-if-it-had-been-ruled-by-a-European-nation/answer/Ratnakar-Sadasyula-%E0%B0%B0%E0%B0%A4%E0%B1%8D%E0%B0%A8%E0%B0%BE%E0%B0%95%E0%B0%B0%E0%B1%8D-%E0%B0%B8%E0%B0%A6%E0%B0%B8%E0%B1%8D%E0%B0%AF%E0%B1%81%E0%B0%B2), enacted by the Tokugawa Shogunate between the mid 17th and mid 19th centuries.Trading with other countries was pretty much limited. Contrary to what many feel, it was not complete isolation, it was more of strict regulation of trade and contact with other nations. Trading was restricted to a few domains, like Tsushima domain with Korea, Nagasaki with China, Matsumae domain with the Ainu people. In fact trade very much prospered during this period, though it was restricted to certain ports. Again while Portuguese were expellen, the Shogunate held talks with the Koreans and Dutch.

While many claim, this was a reaction against the increasing influence of the Spanish, Portugal missionaries, and conversions in Southern Japan, some scholars assert that this was to ensure Japan had more control over it’s foreign policy, and to particulary strengthen the Shogunate. Basically Tokugawa, wanted full control over the trade and revenues, so that none could challenge it. It was more due to the experiences earlier with the Kamakura and Muromachi Shogunates, who had to face problems due to lack of adequate financial resources.

Coming to the missionaries, the Shogunate, was primarily against the Spanish, Portuguese whom they saw as the most aggressive of the lot. They got along well with the Protestant Dutch and English traders, who had managed to keep religion and trade separate. The Dutch pretty much went along with Japan, in order to establish their trading supremacy, as also gain an edge over the Portuguese and Spanish. What is clear is that the isolationist policy was more driven by trading considerations, and Japan’s desire to be at the top of the East Asian hierarchy. So while it reached out to the Dutch and Koreans, it was lukewarm towards China, with both the Ming and Qing dynasties caught up in a survival crisis.

When one speaks of Japanese isolationism, what is clear is that it was more driven by what it perceived to be self interest. It was not a complete cut off from the world, it was more looking at the benefits. It in a way laid the foundation for Japanese outlook, engage with the world, but never forget your roots. In a way the isolationist policy, was also the foundation of their business, where successive Governments favored Japanese business, and put in high entry barriers to foreign businesses.

As for the question of what would have happened to Japan, had it been ruled by a European nation, the answer lies in the Allied Occupation of Japan between 1945–52. Post Japan’s surrender in 1945, Douglas McArthur presided over the Allied occupation of Japan, where it was disarmed and demilitarized, and Western style democracy, economic development was introduced. However even during this phase, while Japan accepted Western ideals, they ensured that they did not lose their distinct identity.

For eg the Allies abolished, the existing Zaibatsu, the cartel system that held a monopoly over industry. However an elite clique of industrialists, felt that such cartelization was necessary for Japanese industry to survive against American, European industry, and they came up with the keiretsu, a much more looser grouping of industies.

Unlike the Allied occupation of Germany, where the existing Government had practically collapsed, Japan still had a functional Government. As McArthur stated, it was more of a SCAP superstructure over the already existing Government machinery. Most political parties in Japane revived quite fast after the war had ended, be it the Communist Party, Japan’s Socialist Party or the more conservative parties.

So Japan’s isolationism, was more of perpetuating it’s dominance, ensuring it’s supremacy at the top of the hierarchy. They did not cut off totally from the outside world, but interacted on a case to case basis, looking at their self interest primarily. For Japanese, maintaining identity, and self interest has been the cornerstone of their thought process. So it has always ensured, that while it engages with the world, it’s roots are never forgotten, and a balance is always maintained between tradition and modernity,
